L = (k/100)·n  

If L is not an integer, round it up to the next integer and take the value in that position.  
If L is an integer, average the values in positions L and L + 1.

*   **Relationship between Mean, Median, and Mode:**

*   **Example:** Scores on a very easy exam. Most students get high scores (e.g., 90-100), but a few who didn't study get very low scores (e.g., 30, 40). These low scores pull the average (mean) down.



#### 3. Skewed to the Right (Positively Skewed)

*   **Description:** The "tail" of the distribution is longer on the right side. This means there are a few unusually high values that pull the mean up. The bulk of the data points are on the lower end of the scale.

### How to Determine Skewness and Describe Distribution Shape

Skewness tells us about the symmetry of a data distribution. There are three main shapes you will be asked to identify.

#### 1. Symmetrical Distribution

*   **Visual Description:** A symmetrical distribution looks like a mirror image of itself if you draw a line down the center. The left and right sides are roughly equal in shape and size. The classic example is a "bell curve."
*   **Relationship of Mean, Median, and Mode:** In a perfectly symmetrical distribution, the mean, median, and mode are all the same value.
    *   **Mean ≈ Median ≈ Mode**



#### 2. Skewed to the Right (Positively Skewed)

*   **Visual Description:** A right-skewed distribution has a long "tail" that extends to the right. The bulk of the data points are clustered on the left side of the graph, with a few high-value outliers pulling the distribution to the right.
*   **Relationship of Mean, Median, and Mode:** The outliers in the right tail pull the **mean** up. The mode remains at the peak of the cluster, and the median lies between the mode and the mean.
    *   **Mode < Median < Mean**
*   **Example:** Income in a country. Most people earn a moderate income (the cluster on the left), but a few billionaires earn extremely high incomes (the long tail on the right), which pulls the average (mean) income up.



#### 3. Skewed to the Left (Negatively Skewed)

*   **Visual Description:** A left-skewed distribution has a long "tail" that extends to the left. The bulk of the data points are clustered on the right side of the graph, with a few low-value outliers pulling the distribution to the left.
*   **Relationship of Mean, Median, and Mode:** The outliers in the left tail pull the **mean** down. The mode remains at the peak of the cluster, and the median lies between the mean and the mode.
    *   **Mean < Median < Mode**
*   **Example:** Age of retirement. Most people retire between 60-70 (the cluster on the right), but a few people retire very early in their 30s or 40s (the long tail on the left), which pulls the average (mean) retirement age down.
